title: Redact
description: A confidential transfer protocol on Ethereum using Fhenix's CoFHE (Coprocessor for Fully Homomorphic Encryption) to encrypt ERC20 token balances on-chain. All FHE computations are handled off-chain by the coprocessor network. Balances are hidden while addresses are fully visible.

properties.name: Confidentiality
properties.notes: Redact uses Fhenix's coprocessor to encrypt ERC20 token balances on-chain. Balances are stored as ciphertexts and transactions modify them using FHE operations. Nobody except the owner can see the plaintext balance or the transaction amounts.

properties.name: Anonymity
properties.notes: Redact operates on Ethereum where all transaction senders and recipients are publicly visible on-chain. The protocol encrypts only token amounts. A normal private transfer transaction in Ethereum would expose the sender and receiver. Hence we consider Redact do not have anonymity on the protocol.

properties.name: Asset privacy
properties.notes: During the encryption step, users must first approve the specific token contract, which is a public on-chain operation. Therefore the contract address is visible to any observer, revealing which asset type is being encrypted.

properties.name: Plausible deniability
properties.notes: The encryption operation is a direct call to the Redact smart contract, making it publicly visible on-chain that the user is interacting with the confidential transfer protocol. There is no way to implement plausible deniability because the sender balance will always be updated to subtract the transferred amount. It will not be clear if it is an addition or subtraction but external observer will be able to see that two Redact accounts have been updated.

properties.name: Number of secrets
properties.notes: Users only need their existing Ethereum wallet (secret #1) to interact with Redact. Permits are temporary EIP-712 signatures derived from the user's wallet key and are not independent secrets requiring separate storage. The balances are encrypted using Fhenix's FHE coprocessor network which manages a public private keypair. The public key is published on anyone can encrypt values. The coprocessor network private key is managed by the network nodes. It can be distributed with threshold encryption mechanisms.

properties.name: Time-to-finality
properties.notes: Redact is deployed on Ethereum using Fhenix's CoFHE coprocessor. The final settlement of transactions occurs on Ethereum, so time to finality defaults to the mainnet economic finality of 12 minutes. Mainnet single-slot finality of 12 seconds has a probabilistic guarantee that there would not be any block reorganization. It is used by wallets and apps to indicate a successful transaction even though in theory the containing block could be reverted. Mainnet economic finality of 12 minutes (2 epochs) is guaranteed by the block checkpointed by Casper the Friendly Finality Gadget (Casper-FFG). At that point reverting the block (and the transaction) would require more than one third of staked ETH to get slashed.

properties.name: Deposit time
properties.notes: No mandatory deposit waiting period. The ERC20 encryption process involves the Fhenix coprocessor which does not clearly state latency or computation times. Therefore we are assuming a 1 minute computation in order to homomorphically encrypt the deposited amount of funds.

properties.name: Withdraw time
properties.notes: No mandatory withdrawal waiting period. Decrypting tokens (withdrawing from Redact) is performed by the Fhenix coprocessor threshold network which would have a specific latency. The latency value is not specified in Redact nor Fhenix documentation but we can assume it would take longer than an encryption. We assume double the time but it needs to be confirmed

properties.name: Censorship resistance
properties.notes: Redact depends on Fhenix's coprocessor network for all FHE operations. There is no clear documentation to join the coprocessor MPC network so it could be considered a permissioned network. Therefore the network could be force to refuse to process operations for specific users or transactions.

properties.name: External network dependence
properties.notes: Redact relies on Fhenix's CoFHE coprocessor network for all FHE computations. The result processor is a permissioned component that validates computation outputs and publishes encrypted results back on-chain. if the whole coprocessor network is taken down, the protocol would be stopped due to not being able to decrypt balances of withdrawal transactions.

properties.name: Escape hatch
properties.notes: There is no escape hatch mechanism in Redact. Encrypted balances can only be decrypted with coprocessor's threshold network. If the CoFHE infrastructure becomes unavailable or censors a user, there is no alternative cryptographic path to recover encrypted funds.

properties.name: Upgradeability
properties.notes: The fhERC20 token standard does not have a governance or upgradeability feature in its code. Redact implementation does not implement a governance mechanism therefore we consider the deployed protocol immutable.

properties.name: Client-side proving
properties.notes: Redact uses FHE computation via the coprocessor rather than client-side ZK proving. All cryptographic operations are processed by the external coprocessor and does not involve proof generation on the user's device.

properties.name: Third-party inspectability
properties.notes: The threshold decryption coprocessor network, operated by Fhenix, can in theory collude and decrypt values. To do this colluders would require a majority of the network participants. This scenario is unlikely to happen in practice. Fhenix is building a strong and resilient FHE network for different apps to use.

properties.name: Implementation maturity
properties.notes: Redact is currently available on public testnet. Nevertheless, a couple of fhERC20 token standards implementation have been deploying on testnets and mainnet networks in order to showcase its functioning.

properties.name: Post-quantum secure
properties.notes: Fhenix's coprocessor documentation does not explicitly indicate the FHE scheme used but in the decryption process there is a mention to Learning With Errors (LWE). Therefore we assume that Fhenix is using a post quantum FHE scheme based on LWE

properties.name: Layer of enforcement
properties.notes: Redact currently has no built-in compliance enforcement mechanism. The protocol focuses solely on confidential transfers without restricting who can participate.

properties.name: Enforcement entities
properties.notes: There is no compliance enforcement entity in Redact. The coprocessor network could collude with an enforcement entity to decrypt specific balances or censor specific addresses.

properties.name: Selective disclosure: viewing entity
properties.notes: Users can issue EIP-712 permits that grant temporary, scoped read access to their encrypted balances. These permits allow the user to voluntarily disclose their confidential balance to any authorized smart contract or third party.

properties.name: Selective disclosure: viewing control
properties.notes: The permit mechanism is a pre-defined EIP-712 structure. Users can grant viewing access but within the fixed permit schema; there is no programmable or custom viewing policy.

properties.name: Cryptographic verifiability
properties.notes: Fhenix's coprocessor network uses zero knowledge proofs alongside FHE to ensure that all coprocessor computations are correct and verifiable on-chain without revealing plaintext data.

properties.name: Open source
properties.notes: The Fhenix CoFHE libraries, Redact repository and related tooling are available on GitHub.

properties.name: Scalable state
properties.notes: Redact uses an account-based model where each user's encrypted balance is stored as a mapping within the smart contract storage. State grows proportionally to the number of accounts rather than to the number of transactions.

properties.name: Client-side indexing
properties.notes: Since Redact uses an account-based model with encrypted balances stored directly as contract state, users can retrieve their encrypted balance directly from the contract without scanning past blockchain events.

properties.name: Private state model
properties.notes: Each Redact account holds an encrypted balance stored directly in the smart contract. The balance ciphertext is updated in-place on each transfer

properties.name: On-chain storage
properties.notes: All encrypted balances (FHE ciphertexts) are stored on-chain and ciphertext pointers related to specific accounts are saved within the Redact smart contract state.

properties.name: Access to DeFi
properties.notes: fhERC20 tokens are encrypted and cannot be used directly with standard DeFi protocols. Users must decrypt (unshield) their tokens back to standard ERC20 form before accessing any external DeFi ecosystem.

properties.name: Programmability / Generality
properties.notes: Redact is designed as a confidential payment protocol for ERC20 token transfers. The core operations are encrypting tokens, transferring confidential balances, and decrypting tokens. There is no support for arbitrary private smart contract logic.
